name: Nemotron 3 Ultra
description: NVIDIA's open frontier reasoning model, released 2026-06-04 to complete the Nemotron 3 rollout (Nano Dec 2025, Super Mar 2026): a 550B total / 55B active LatentMoE hybrid Mamba-Transformer under OpenMDW-1.1 with open weights, training data, and recipes. 1M-token context, 71.9% SWE-bench Verified (vendor), Artificial Analysis Index 48 — the top-scoring US open-weight model — with ~140 tok/s decode and the best non-hallucination score in its comparison set (78.7 AA-Omniscience).

evidence.value: SWE-bench Verified 71.9, IOI 2025 570.0 (vendor-reported)
SWE-bench Verified 65.0-70.4 across five agent harnesses in independent runs, slightly below the 71.9 vendor peak; Terminal-Bench 2.1 56.4 (vendor-stated)

task_accuracy_code.methodology: Vendor coding benchmarks cross-checked against independent multi-harness SWE-bench reproductions

evidence.source: Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (via Digital Applied)
evidence.value: Intelligence Index 48 (#9 of 89): highest-scoring US open-weight model, 6 points behind open leader Kimi K2.6 (54)
PinchBench 90.0 held-out; post-trained with SFT, RLVR, and Multi-teacher On-Policy Distillation for long-running agent reasoning

task_accuracy_reasoning.methodology: Independent aggregate intelligence index plus vendor reasoning benchmarks; some vendor numbers await third-party replication

evidence.value: AA-Omniscience 78.7, the highest non-hallucination score in its comparison set; RULER 94.7 at 1M tokens

task_accuracy_general.methodology: Knowledge and long-context retrieval benchmarks from launch materials, corroborated by independent press analysis

evidence.value: Stable results across agent harnesses but notably verbose: ~2.3x more output tokens than peer models

output_consistency.methodology: Cross-harness benchmark variance and community reports; only one month of production usage available
output_consistency.notes: Launch-recency: limited repeated-run data from the community so far

evidence.value: 140.3 tokens/s output (#7 of 89 models) with 1.33s time-to-first-token; several times faster than Kimi K2.6 (50-100 tok/s)
5.9x throughput vs GLM-5.1 and 4.8x vs Kimi K2.6 at 8K input / 64K output (vendor)

latency_p50.methodology: Independent hosted-endpoint speed measurements plus vendor throughput comparisons

latency_p95.value: Provider-dependent; fast decode limits tail latency, but reasoning verbosity (~2.3x peer token counts) lengthens end-to-end task time

evidence.value: High decode speed offset by 2.3x output-token verbosity on reasoning tasks; per-provider tail latencies not yet broadly characterized

latency_p95.methodology: Qualitative assessment from early independent benchmarking; p95 distributions not yet published one month post-launch

evidence.value: 1M-token context window; RULER 94.7 at 1M tokens (some hosts serve reduced limits)

context_window.methodology: Official specification from model card; note that individual API providers may cap served context

evidence.value: Day-zero availability across 25+ platforms (NVIDIA NIM, OpenRouter, Together AI, Fireworks, Perplexity, SageMaker JumpStart) plus self-hosting redundancy

uptime.methodology: Multi-provider availability assessment; only one month of hosted operating history

performance_reliability.notes: Strongest US open-weight model on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (48, #9 overall) with class-leading decode speed (~140 tok/s) and a 1M-token context. Vendor benchmark peaks (SWE-bench 71.9) run a few points above independent multi-harness reproductions (65.0-70.4). Verbosity (~2.3x peer output tokens) is the main efficiency caveat.

evidence.value: Safety post-training documented; no published third-party injection red-team results one month post-launch

prompt_injection_resistance.methodology: Review of vendor safety documentation; independent OWASP LLM01 testing not yet available for this release

evidence.value: NVIDIA ships a companion Nemotron 3.5 Content Safety model (4B, 23 safety categories, 12 languages, released 2026-06-02); base alignment is removable downstream given open weights

jailbreak_resistance.methodology: Assessment of vendor guardrail stack; accounts for open-weight modifiability and absence of independent adversarial evaluations

evidence.value: Open weights allow fully self-hosted deployment with complete data control; hosted access follows each provider's data handling terms

data_leakage_prevention.methodology: Analysis of deployment options: self-hosting gives full data isolation, hosted routes depend on provider policies

evidence.value: Safety-aligned release with documented post-training; pairs with the Nemotron 3.5 Content Safety classifier covering 23 categories across 12 languages

output_safety.methodology: Review of default-weight safety behavior and the vendor's companion moderation stack

evidence.value: NVIDIA NIM and major hosts provide API-key authentication, HTTPS, and rate limiting via OpenAI-compatible endpoints

api_security.methodology: Review of API security features on NVIDIA NIM and principal third-party hosts

security.notes: Reasonable default safety stack including a dedicated companion content-safety model, but almost no independent red-team literature exists yet given the June 2026 launch. Open weights shift guardrail responsibility to deployers who fine-tune.

data_residency.value: Deployment-dependent: US-jurisdiction NVIDIA NIM, 25+ third-party hosts, or self-hosted in any region

evidence.value: Day-zero availability on OpenRouter, NVIDIA NIM, Together AI, Fireworks, Perplexity, and Amazon SageMaker JumpStart; OpenMDW weights allow deployment anywhere

data_residency.methodology: Review of hosting options and open-weight licensing; residency is fully controllable via self-hosting

evidence.value: NVIDIA hosted services do not train on API data by default; self-hosting removes the concern entirely

training_data_optout.methodology: Analysis of NVIDIA hosted-service terms plus the self-hosting option

data_retention.value: Per host policy on managed endpoints; zero external retention when self-hosted

evidence.value: Hosted retention follows NVIDIA or third-party host policies; open weights make retention fully deployment-dependent

data_retention.methodology: Review of hosted-platform retention policies across the deployment spectrum

evidence.value: Customer responsible for PII redaction; NVIDIA documents data provenance and provides NeMo Guardrails tooling in the surrounding stack

pii_handling.methodology: Review of data protection capabilities and deployer responsibilities

evidence.value: NVIDIA infrastructure holds SOC 2 Type II and GDPR programs; no model-service HIPAA/FedRAMP path — regulated deployments inherit certifications from the chosen host (e.g., SageMaker) or self-hosted environment

compliance_certifications.methodology: Verification of provider infrastructure certifications versus model-service-level compliance

evidence.value: No formal zero-retention guarantee on hosted endpoints; self-hosting provides true zero external retention

zero_data_retention.methodology: Review of data handling across NVIDIA NIM, third-party hosts, and self-hosting

privacy_compliance.notes: US-jurisdiction provider with unusually flexible residency thanks to open weights and 25+ hosts, including hyperscaler routes (SageMaker JumpStart) that carry their own certifications. No model-level HIPAA/FedRAMP; regulated buyers should deploy via certified infrastructure.

evidence.value: Reasoning model exposing chain-of-thought traces; fully inspectable weights and recipes when self-hosted

explainability.methodology: Evaluation of reasoning-trace accessibility and weight/recipe inspectability

evidence.value: 78.7 on AA-Omniscience — the highest non-hallucination score in its comparison set

hallucination_rate.methodology: Non-hallucination benchmark results from launch materials, pending broader independent replication

evidence.value: NVIDIA Responsible AI documentation and data provenance disclosed; independent bias audits not yet published for this release

bias_fairness.methodology: Review of vendor responsible-AI disclosures; third-party fairness evaluations pending given launch recency

evidence.value: Expresses uncertainty within reasoning traces; calibration data not yet independently characterized

uncertainty_quantification.methodology: Qualitative assessment of confidence expression in outputs

evidence.value: Detailed cards for BF16, Base-BF16, NVFP4, and GenRM variants covering architecture (LatentMoE, MTP), training phases, data cutoffs (pre-train Sep 2025, post-train May 2026), and deployment recipes

model_card_quality.methodology: Review of model card and technical documentation completeness

evidence.value: Openly released training corpora: 20T-token pre-training mix documented (Nemotron-CC, Common Crawl snapshots CC-MAIN-2013-20 through 2025-13), plus 10M SFT samples and 1M RL tasks published under OpenMDW-1.1

training_data_transparency.methodology: Review of published training datasets and recipes; best-in-class disclosure among frontier-scale models

evidence.value: Safety alignment in released weights plus the companion Nemotron 3.5 Content Safety 4B classifier; removable by downstream fine-tuning

guardrails.methodology: Analysis of built-in safety mechanisms in default weights and companion tooling

trust_transparency.notes: Transparency is this release's standout: weights, 20T-token training data, and recipes are all published under OpenMDW-1.1 — materially beyond typical open-weight disclosure. Independent bias and calibration audits are still pending one month post-launch.

evidence.value: OpenAI-compatible endpoints via NVIDIA NIM and 25+ hosts with function calling and reasoning controls

api_design_quality.methodology: Review of API design and feature completeness across NIM and principal hosts

evidence.value: Day-zero support in vLLM, SGLang, and TensorRT-LLM; Hugging Face Transformers integration; single NVFP4 checkpoint spans Ampere through Blackwell

sdk_quality.methodology: Review of inference-framework support and deployment tooling at launch

evidence.value: Predictable staged rollout: Nemotron 3 Nano (Dec 2025), Super 120B-A12B (2026-03-11), Ultra 550B-A55B (2026-06-04)
Open weights remain permanently downloadable, softening deprecation risk; note NVIDIA retired the prior Llama-3.1-based Nemotron line within about a year

versioning_policy.methodology: Review of release cadence, family roadmap execution, and weight-availability guarantees

evidence.value: Usage dashboards on NIM and third-party hosts; full observability when self-hosting on vLLM/SGLang/TRT-LLM stacks

monitoring_observability.methodology: Review of monitoring tools across deployment options

evidence.value: Enterprise support with SLAs available via NVIDIA AI Enterprise; strong developer documentation and community channels

support_quality.methodology: Assessment of enterprise support tiers, documentation, and community responsiveness

evidence.value: Complete family ladder (Nano 30B-A3B, Super 120B-A12B, Ultra 550B-A55B) with early community quantizations (e.g., Unsloth GGUF); derivative ecosystem still nascent one month post-launch

ecosystem_maturity.methodology: Analysis of derivative models, third-party hosting breadth, and tooling integrations; scored conservatively given launch recency

evidence.value: OpenMDW License 1.1 (Linux Foundation): permissive, covers weights, data, and recipes, with patent-termination clause; less legally familiar to enterprises than Apache 2.0

license_terms.methodology: Review of licensing terms and restrictions

operational_excellence.notes: Strong launch operations: day-zero support across 25+ hosts and all major inference frameworks, plus a single NVFP4 checkpoint spanning Ampere to Blackwell. Ecosystem and monitoring scores held conservative pending more production history; OpenMDW-1.1 is permissive but newer than Apache 2.0.

code-generation.notes: 71.9% SWE-bench Verified (vendor; 65.0-70.4 independent) with fast decode suits agentic coding; verbosity raises per-task output cost.

customer-support.notes: Capable but text-only, verbose, and primarily English-optimized; oversized for most support tiers.

content-creation.notes: Solid structured writing; reasoning verbosity requires prompt discipline for concise content.

data-analysis.notes: 1M-token context with RULER 94.7 handles very large datasets and logs; text-only, so charts/images need preprocessing.

research-assistant.notes: 1M context plus the best non-hallucination score in its set (78.7 AA-Omniscience) suits long-document research and long-running agents.

legal-compliance.notes: Compliance is deployment-dependent: viable via certified hosts (e.g., SageMaker) or self-hosting; no model-service certifications.

healthcare.notes: No HIPAA path on NVIDIA-hosted endpoints; deploy in compliant infrastructure via open weights.

financial-analysis.notes: Strong reasoning and long-context document processing; independent calibration data still limited.

education.notes: Clear reasoning traces aid tutoring, but verbosity and English-first coverage limit fit versus multilingual alternatives.

creative-writing.notes: Reasoning-optimized rather than prose-optimized; competent but not distinctive creative output.

strengths: Top-scoring US open-weight model: Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index 48 (#9 of 89)
Radical transparency: weights, 20T-token training data, and recipes all released under OpenMDW-1.1
1M-token context with RULER 94.7 at full length
Fast for its class: ~140 tok/s decode, 1.33s TTFT; vendor-reported 4.8-5.9x throughput vs open peers
Best non-hallucination score in its comparison set (78.7 AA-Omniscience)
Single NVFP4 checkpoint runs across Ampere, Hopper, and Blackwell; day-zero vLLM/SGLang/TRT-LLM support
Completes a predictable family ladder (Nano 30B-A3B, Super 120B-A12B, Ultra 550B-A55B)
limitations: Very verbose: ~2.3x more output tokens than peers, inflating cost and end-to-end latency on reasoning tasks
Trails the open-weight leader Kimi K2.6 by 6 Intelligence Index points (48 vs 54)
Vendor benchmark peaks exceed independent reproductions (SWE-bench 71.9 vendor vs 65.0-70.4 across harnesses)
Text-only: no image, audio, or video input
Launched 2026-06-04: minimal independent red-teaming, bias audits, or production track record
550B total parameters require serious multi-GPU infrastructure to self-host
OpenMDW-1.1 license is permissive but less familiar to enterprise legal teams than Apache 2.0
best_for: Long-running autonomous agents needing 1M-token context and low hallucination
Organizations requiring full-stack auditability (open weights, data, and recipes)
Agentic coding on US-jurisdiction open infrastructure
NVIDIA-based self-hosting from Ampere to Blackwell via one NVFP4 checkpoint
Long-document analysis and retrieval at extreme context lengths
not_recommended_for: Cost-sensitive workloads where output-token verbosity dominates spend
Regulated deployments via NVIDIA-hosted endpoints alone (no HIPAA/FedRAMP model-service path)
Teams without GPU infrastructure that also need on-prem deployment
High-assurance uses requiring an established independent security track record

pricing.input: Free weights (OpenMDW-1.1); hosted ~$0.50 per 1M tokens (OpenRouter reference)
pricing.output: Hosted ~$2.20 per 1M tokens (OpenRouter reference); free-tier variant available
pricing.notes: Self-hosting is infrastructure-cost-only. Budget for ~2.3x peer output-token verbosity on reasoning tasks, which erodes headline per-token savings. Rates vary by host.

metadata.architecture: LatentMoE hybrid Mamba-Transformer: interleaved Mamba-2, MoE (512 experts, top-22 active), and select attention layers across 108 layers, with Multi-Token Prediction and NVFP4 4-bit pretraining
